metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `--singleton` flag semantics in the Implementation CLI spec section (e.g., 'bypasses `pack_prerequisite_bins` and issues one API call per prerequisite block, for baseline generation') so the executor can implement and invoke the first-slice proving command without inference.
evidence: Proving command: `.venv/bin/python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing --singleton --write-baseline ...`
CLI spec only names `--prerequisite-packing`; `--singleton` appears exclusively in step 1 and the Binding baseline section with no definition of its behavior
findings.finding: The `--singleton` flag is required by the first-slice proving command but its semantics (e.g., 'run each prerequisite in its own call, skipping bin packing') are never defined in the Implementation section or any Binding section. An executor reading the CLI spec sees only `--prerequisite-packing`; `--singleton` must be inferred, making the first-slice proving command un-implementable without guesswork.
findings.location: Binding implementation sequence step 1 / first-slice proving command; Implementation CLI spec paragraph ('Add separate opt-in `prerequisite_packing_spot_eval` and a module CLI entry point `--prerequisite-packing`')
scenarios: Executor implements the CLI, adds `--prerequisite-packing`, but does not know what `--singleton` should do — whether it disables batching, limits corpus size, changes the runner, or something else. The first-slice gate cannot be run.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation CLI paragraph: '`--singleton` bypasses `pack_prerequisite_bins` and issues one focused-finder call per prerequisite block; it is intended only for baseline generation and fidelity comparison against packed runs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the single undecorated `fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing` line in the Verification section with the two explicit proving commands (slice-1 `--singleton --write-baseline` and slice-2 `--baseline <path>`) that match the Binding implementation sequence and Binding comparative baseline definitions.
evidence: Verification: 'Run `.venv/bin/python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing`; require exit 0'
Binding implementation sequence: 'The first-slice proving command is `.venv/bin/python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing --singleton --write-baseline ...`'
Binding implementation sequence: 'The second-slice proving command is `.venv/bin/python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing --baseline ...`'
Binding comparative baseline: 'Acceptance criterion: this command exits 0 and its report contains no `recall regressed from singleton` or `false acceptance regressed from singleton` failure'
findings.finding: The Verification section instructs running `fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing` as a single undecorated command, but the Binding implementation sequence and Binding comparative baseline sections define two mandatory proving commands with distinct flags (`--singleton --write-baseline` for slice 1; `--baseline <path>` for slice 2). An implementer following only the Verification section would skip the baseline-comparative gate entirely.
findings.location: Verification section (fidelity_spot_eval command) vs Binding implementation sequence (first/second slice proving commands) vs Binding comparative baseline (second-slice command definition)
scenarios: Implementer runs only the undecorated command in Verification, marks the story done, and never runs the baseline-comparative gate required by Binding comparative baseline, silently skipping regression protection.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the fidelity_spot_eval line in Verification with:
```
Run `.venv/bin/python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing --singleton --write-baseline src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/plan-review-prerequisite-packing.baseline.json` (slice 1); require exit 0.
Run `.venv/bin/python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing --baseline src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/plan-review-prerequisite-packing.baseline.json` (slice 2); require exit 0 with no regression failures.
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the blocking posture and `default_posture` (e.g. `blocking` or `advisory`) of the prerequisite-consistency criterion in the plan, so `_criteria.threshold_for` can correctly route verified incompatibilities to block or advisory decisions.
evidence: 'Register one prerequisite-consistency criterion' — no posture, threshold, or default_posture is specified for it
orchestrator.pass3_over_findings calls _criteria.threshold_for(criteria, crit_by_id, gate='plan_review') which reads default_posture from the registry

Confirmed in code: the plan's focused pass3_over_findings call is 'pass the same boolean to both general and focused pass3_over_findings' — so the threshold_for lookup is the same registry-driven dispatch
findings.finding: The plan never defines the blocking posture, threshold, or default_posture of the registered prerequisite-consistency criterion. Since `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings` (and the focused variant) dispatches `threshold_for(criteria)` against `registry.by_id()`, the prerequisite-consistency criterion must be registered with a defined posture. Without it, `_criteria.threshold_for` falls back to the default (advisory), meaning even a verified concrete incompatibility cannot become a blocking finding regardless of its severity attributes.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Register one prerequisite-consistency criterion' and 'Binding focused prompt and failure contracts: The only criterion ID is the registered prerequisite-consistency criterion'
scenarios: A prerequisite plan explicitly states it will NOT deliver interface X, and the subject plan depends on interface X. The LLM finder correctly returns a `finding` disposition. The verifier sets `prerequisite_attribution_valid=yes`. But because the prerequisite-consistency criterion has no defined posture, pass3_over_findings treats it as advisory — the incompatibility becomes advisory-only, never blocking, even if severity attributes are high.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Register one prerequisite-consistency criterion' line: define `default_posture: blocking` (since a confirmed prerequisite incompatibility should block by default) and `block_threshold: 0.5` (or match the existing blocking criteria), and include this criterion in the criteria registry entry alongside its `exec: 1-TURN` tier.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion such as: 'P8 short-circuit and drift_refresh paths pass no prerequisite coverage; `coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate")` evaluates false and their existing verdicts remain unchanged.'
evidence: Binding comparative baseline: 'The P8 short-circuit and `drift_refresh` call sites pass no prerequisite coverage by design; `coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate")` is false on those paths and their existing verdicts remain unchanged.'
ABSENCE: No AC in the main Acceptance Criteria list addresses P8/drift_refresh path behavior.
findings.finding: The P8-short-circuit and drift_refresh behavioral constraint — 'coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate") is false on those paths and their existing verdicts remain unchanged' — is defined in Binding comparative baseline as binding operational behavior but has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion in the main AC list, leaving it unverifiable at story close.
findings.location: Binding comparative baseline section (last paragraph) vs Acceptance Criteria section
scenarios: An implementer accidentally passes prerequisite coverage into P8/drift_refresh paths and forces incorrect INDETERMINATE verdicts on those routes; story closes without catching the regression because no AC covers it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion covering the structured-event observability requirement: `plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate` is emitted with the required fields for every indeterminate record, counters are partitioned by reason code, and emitted events contain no prerequisite plan text.
evidence: Binding operational behavior: 'Emit structured event `plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate` with ticket ID, prerequisite ID, reason code, model, attempt count, and bin size for every evaluation-error, output-invalid, or attribution-invalid record.'

Binding focused prompt and failure contracts: 'Add acceptance coverage for observability: tests assert each evaluation-error, output-invalid, and attribution-invalid record emits `plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate`...'
ABSENCE: No entry in the 14-item main Acceptance Criteria list references this event or its observability properties.
findings.finding: The structured-event observability requirement — emit `plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate` with ticket ID, prerequisite ID, reason code, model, attempt count, and bin size for every evaluation-error, output-invalid, or attribution-invalid record — is declared binding in both Binding operational behavior and Binding focused prompt and failure contracts (with a test requirement), but no AC in the main Acceptance Criteria list covers this deliverable.
findings.location: Binding operational behavior section; Binding focused prompt and failure contracts section (observability paragraph) vs Acceptance Criteria section
scenarios: Observability instrumentation is omitted or misconfigured; story closes as accepted because no main AC requires it, leaving production operations blind to indeterminate-disposition events.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly in the `_prerequisite_coverage_model()` definition block whether the `Finding` type inside `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord` is a subtype of `P1Finding` with an added required `prerequisite_id: str` field, and name that inner model class.
evidence: Plan: 'Define `_prerequisite_coverage_model()` in `passes.py` with outer `PrerequisiteCoverageOutput { records: list[PrerequisiteCoverageRecord] }`. Each record is `{prerequisite_id: canonical string, disposition: consistent|finding|indeterminate, findings: list[Finding], reason_code?: ..., detail?: string}`'

Plan: 'each finding uses the existing Pass-1 finding fields `finding`, `criteria`, `location`, `evidence`, `scenarios`, `impact`, `checklist_item`, and `suggested_fix`, plus required `prerequisite_id`'
Existing P1Finding in passes.py (lines 94-114) has no `prerequisite_id` field — the extension is never named as the model factory's `Finding` type
No `Finding` type definition exists in the plan's `_prerequisite_coverage_model()` body
findings.finding: The `Finding` type referenced inside `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord.findings: list[Finding]` is never defined in the schema specification block. The plan names the outer model as `PrerequisiteCoverageOutput { records: list[PrerequisiteCoverageRecord] }` and states each record carries `findings: list[Finding]`, but never defines what `Finding` means here: specifically, whether it is the existing `P1Finding` model from `passes.py`, an extended subtype that adds the required `prerequisite_id` field, or a new standalone type. The Binding focused prompt section says each finding uses 'the existing Pass-1 finding fields ... plus required `prerequisite_id` equal to the enclosing record ID', but this description is in a different section from `_prerequisite_coverage_model()` definition and is not expressed as a model shape.
findings.location: Implementation section — `_prerequisite_coverage_model()` definition; Binding focused prompt and failure contracts section
scenarios: Implementer writes _prerequisite_coverage_model() and must choose: reuse P1Finding (missing prerequisite_id), create a subclass (not specified), or create a fresh type (duplication). Any choice is a guess.
Schema validation at contract registration will be silent about the prerequisite_id field unless the model explicitly includes it — a wrong choice silently accepts output without the required field.
findings.suggested_fix: In the `_prerequisite_coverage_model()` specification, define a local `PrerequisiteFinding` class inheriting/mirroring `P1Finding` fields plus `prerequisite_id: str = Field(description='Authoritative canonical prerequisite ticket ID')`, and update the schema block to use `findings: list[PrerequisiteFinding]`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'tail conflict' precisely in the plan (e.g., 'a prerequisite that is the sole block in its greedy-packed bin because adding it would exceed the budget of the preceding bin') and add that definition adjacent to its first normative use in the batching section.
evidence: Plan: 'A tail conflict is evaluated identically to a singleton call'
Plan: 'Add eight or more gold sets of at least three prerequisites: two all-consistent, two single-conflict, two tail-conflict, one attribution-confusion, and one multi-bin'
No definition of 'tail conflict' appears anywhere in the plan
The term does not appear in any existing codebase file (search returned no matches outside cache files)
findings.finding: The term 'tail conflict' is used normatively in two places — 'stable bins contain whole blocks only ... A tail conflict is evaluated identically to a singleton call' (Implementation, batching section) and 'two tail-conflict' gold sets in the fidelity spec (Implementation, fidelity section) — but is never defined anywhere in the plan. An implementer must infer its meaning (likely: a prerequisite that lands alone in the last bin after greedy packing, because it doesn't fit with earlier blocks) without any authoritative definition.
findings.location: Implementation section — batching paragraph ('A tail conflict is evaluated identically to a singleton call'); fidelity paragraph ('two tail-conflict gold sets')
scenarios: Implementer constructs gold sets with the wrong topology (e.g., treating 'tail conflict' as a multi-prerequisite conflict at the end of a list rather than a packing edge case), making the gold evaluation miss the intended edge case.
The bin-splitting code handles singleton escalation but not the specific tail-conflict scenario because it wasn't recognized as distinct.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'A tail conflict is a prerequisite block that ends up as the only element in its bin after greedy packing — it could not fit into the prior bin and has no subsequent blocks to share a bin with. It is structurally equivalent to a singleton call and must be verified to route through the singleton escalation path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the focused verifier batching algorithm explicitly — either by naming a `pack_prerequisite_verifier_bins(records, *, subject_plan, system_prompt, model, per_record_output_tokens, headroom)` function in `sizing.py` with its signature, or by explicitly stating that `pack_prerequisite_bins` is reused with `PrerequisiteBlock` constructed from finding-records and documenting the token charge formula.
evidence: Plan specifies finder packing: 'Define `pack_prerequisite_bins(blocks: Sequence[PrerequisiteBlock], *, subject_plan: str, system_prompt: str, model: str | None, per_block_output_tokens: int = PER_FINDING_VERIFY_TOKENS, headroom: float = DEFAULT_VERIFY_WINDOW_HEADROOM) -> tuple[list[list[PrerequisiteBlock]], list[PrerequisiteBlock]]`'
Plan for verifier batching only says: 'Multiple whole focused records may share a call within budget'
Plan: 'Size the actual focused prompt topology ... through a prerequisite-specific adapter in `sizing.py`' — singular adapter, not clear if shared by finder and verifier
No verifier packing function signature is defined anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The focused verifier batching algorithm is not specified. The plan fully defines `pack_prerequisite_bins` for the focused finder path (Binding step 4, with full function signature, inputs, outputs, and greedy algorithm), but for the focused verifier side it only states 'Multiple whole focused records may share a call within budget' and 'Focused verifier context-limit recovery uses the same whole-record algorithm as the finder: split the stable finding-record list into contiguous halves'. No packing function, sizing adapter call, token budget computation, or function name is given for grouping verifier finding-records into calls. The plan says to use a 'prerequisite-specific adapter in `sizing.py`' for finder sizing but doesn't state whether that same adapter or a separate one handles verifier sizing.
findings.location: Implementation section — verifier batching paragraph ('Multiple whole focused records may share a call within budget'); Binding focused prompt and failure contracts — verifier context-limit recovery
scenarios: Implementer writes the verifier batching ad-hoc without a named sizing function, leading to token budget computation that drifts from the finder's headroom/reserve constants.
The verifier packs finding-records by per-record output token estimates that differ from what the finder uses per block, creating window-limit errors in production.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the `corpus_digest` computation algorithm in the Binding comparative baseline section (e.g., 'SHA-256 of the UTF-8 JSON serialization of the sorted gold-set list as loaded at evaluation time, hex-encoded').
evidence: 'corpus_digest' is named as a required baseline key and is explicitly validated on second-slice invocation
No hashing algorithm, input set, or serialization order is specified anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The `corpus_digest` field required in the baseline JSON by the second-slice validator (`--baseline` path) is specified to exist and be validated, but no algorithm for computing it is defined. The executor must choose a digest scheme (hash of corpus file contents? sorted gold IDs? fixture path list?), which risks interoperability failures between `--write-baseline` and `--baseline` invocations across environments.
findings.location: Binding comparative baseline section: 'atomically writes JSON keys `recall`, `false_accept`, `coverage_completeness`, `prerequisite_attribution_error_rate`, `model`, `corpus_digest`, and `recorded_at`'; 'It validates the baseline schema and corpus digest'
scenarios: Two implementers or CI runs compute `corpus_digest` differently (e.g., hash of file path vs. hash of file content vs. hash of sorted gold IDs). The second-slice validator rejects a valid baseline, or accepts a stale one when corpus changes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete contract test (the `upstream_contract` test already referenced) that asserts the exact parameter name `execution_review` in `pass3_over_findings`'s signature, and define a fallback default (e.g., `execution_review=False`) so the focused path degrades gracefully if upstream drifts.
evidence: Current `pass3_over_findings` signature in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:678`: `def pass3_over_findings(findings, verifs)` — no `execution_review` parameter
Current `pass3_over_findings` in `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py:579`: `def pass3_over_findings(findings, verifs, *, threshold_for, impact_fn=None)` — no `execution_review` parameter
No match for `execution_review` anywhere in `src/rebar/llm/` (confirmed via search)
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Determine `execution_review` once from the review-phase snapshot supplied by `5967` and pass the same boolean to both general and focused `pass3_over_findings(..., execution_review=execution_review)` calls, applying the 0.8 execution floor consistently.' The current `pass3_over_findings` in `orchestrator.py:678` and `review_kernel/decide.py:579` has no `execution_review` parameter. The plan gates on `5967` closing and merging, but does not include a fallback behavior if `execution_review` is absent (e.g., because the upstream merged with a different call signature). The 'At implementation start, verify both symbols and contract tests exist; if either differs, stop as upstream contract drift' instruction provides a stop condition but not a defined default for a missing parameter.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Determine execution_review once from the review-phase snapshot supplied by 5967 and pass the same boolean to both general and focused pass3_over_findings(..., execution_review=execution_review) calls'
scenarios: If `5967` delivers `pass3_over_findings` with a different parameter name (e.g., `is_execution_review` or `execution_mode`), this plan's calls will fail at runtime with no graceful degradation path.

If `5967` merges with `execution_review` as a keyword-only argument with a default, the 'stop as upstream contract drift' instruction may be too strict — the call would still work but produce different threshold behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a `pack_verifier_records(records, ...)` function (or extend the prerequisite sizing adapter) with an explicit greedy bin-packing algorithm for focused verifier finding records, analogous to `pack_prerequisite_bins`, so the verifier call count is O(bins) not O(findings).
evidence: 'Multiple whole focused records may share a call within budget' — intent stated but algorithm absent

'pack_prerequisite_bins' is defined with a complete greedy algorithm in Binding implementation sequence step 4, but no analogous `pack_verifier_records` or similar function is defined for the verifier

'Size the actual focused prompt topology, including system prompt, subject plan, prerequisite or finding blocks, output reserve, and headroom, through a prerequisite-specific adapter in `sizing.py`' — sizing is specified but grouping/packing strategy for verifier finding records is not
findings.finding: The verifier-side call packing algorithm is not specified: `pack_prerequisite_bins` is fully defined for finder calls, but no analogous packing function or algorithm is defined for grouped verifier calls. The plan states 'Multiple whole focused records may share a call within budget' and delegates sizing to 'a prerequisite-specific adapter in `sizing.py`', but leaves the implementation strategy implicit. Without a concrete packing algorithm, the implementation is likely to default to one LLM call per finding record, producing an O(F) call count where F is the total number of focused findings across all prerequisites — an N+1 pattern.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Size the actual focused prompt topology… through a prerequisite-specific adapter in `sizing.py`' and 'Multiple whole focused records may share a call within budget'
scenarios: A ticket with 10 prerequisites each producing 5 findings yields 50 focused verifier records; without packing, 50 separate LLM calls are made instead of the handful that bin-packing would produce.
A reviewer adds precise but numerous incompatibility findings; each gets its own verifier call, multiplying API cost linearly with finding count.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Binding implementation sequence step 4 (or a new step): define `pack_verifier_records(records: Sequence[VerifierRecord], *, subject_plan: str, system_prompt: str, model: str | None, per_record_output_tokens: int = PER_FINDING_VERIFY_TOKENS, headroom: float = DEFAULT_VERIFY_WINDOW_HEADROOM) -> tuple[list[list[VerifierRecord]], list[VerifierRecord]]` in `sizing.py`, using the same greedy-append-while-under-limit strategy as `pack_prerequisite_bins`, ordering by stable `(prerequisite_id, finding_index)` order. Reference this function from the verifier runner section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact signature and parameter type for the `_build_verification_output` extra-binary-fields extension (e.g., `extra_binary_fields: dict[str, tuple[type, FieldInfo]] | None = None`) and show how the two non-plan-review callers safely pass `None` without schema change.
evidence: Current `_build_verification_output` signature at `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py:108`: `def _build_verification_output(forbid: Any, severity_cls: type) -> type:` — no extra-binary-fields parameter
Three callers at lines 180, 261, 350 of `verify_models.py`: `return _build_verification_output(forbid, SeverityAttrs)` — all positional, no keyword args
The plan says `verification_model` and `code_review_verification_model` 'pass no extra field and remain schema-identical' — but the extension mechanism is unspecified
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Reuse the registered index-keyed `plan_review_verification` contract for focused Pass 2' and 'Extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument and have only `plan_review_verification_model` pass `prerequisite_attribution_valid` with default `na`.' The current `_build_verification_output` in `verify_models.py` has signature `(forbid, severity_cls)` — no extra-binary-fields parameter. The plan's prescription depends on Python's ability to add an optional keyword argument without breaking the three existing callers (`verification_model`, `plan_review_verification_model`, `code_review_verification_model`). While this is mechanically possible, the plan does not specify what the new parameter's type is (a dict? a list of field tuples?), what happens if both the `severity_cls` path and the `extra-binary-fields` path try to add the same field name, or how the two code-review callers safely pass nothing. A reviewer cannot verify that this extension is sound without those specifics.
findings.location: Implementation section — `_build_verification_output` extension
scenarios: If the extra-binary-fields parameter is a dict, the caller must construct a field spec (name, type, default, description) — the plan does not specify this protocol.

If a future refactor of `_build_binary` is needed to accommodate the new field, the plan's constraint 'do not add the field to shared _build_binary' creates a coupling risk with the extension mechanism.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation section: 'The new signature is `_build_verification_output(forbid, severity_cls, *, extra_binary_fields: dict[str, Any] | None = None) -> type`; callers that pass `None` (or omit it) produce a model byte-identical to the current schema; only `plan_review_verification_model` passes `{"prerequisite_attribution_valid": (str, Field(default="na", description="yes|no|na"))}`.'

findings.finding: The leaf introduces two independent decide sub-pipelines — 'independently reshape and decide each domain' with separate pass3_over_findings calls for general and focused arrays, and explicit concatenation of general_decided + focused_decided — which the parent epic explicitly prohibits: 'No parallel decide pipeline is introduced.'
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Preserve two non-overlapping verification index domains… In plan_review_decide, independently reshape and decide each domain… Concatenate general_decided + focused_decided only after both decide calls return… pass the same boolean to both general and focused pass3_over_findings(…) calls'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the two-domain separate-decide architecture. Instead, fold prerequisite findings (with their prerequisite_id annotations and attribution fields) into the existing single decide pass alongside general findings. Attribution-invalid handling (converting a record to indeterminate) can be done as a pre-decide normalization step before a single unified pass3_over_findings call, preserving the epic's single-pipeline constraint.

evidence: description is 24039 chars (> 24000)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback path or feature flag for the `finalize_verdict` INDETERMINATE branch and the `plan_review_decide` blocking-to-indeterminate migration, or justify in the plan why a code-revert is the accepted back-out strategy.
evidence: Plan says 'P8 short-circuit and drift_refresh call sites pass no prerequisite coverage by design' — existing callers are guarded by data, but no rollback procedure or feature flag is described for reverting the verdict-assembly change itself if it causes issues in production.
T4 rubric requires 'an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract — acknowledgement alone is insufficient'; the plan acknowledges guarding but provides no reversal mechanism.
findings.finding: The behavioral changes to `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` (new INDETERMINATE branch inserted before existing branches) and to `plan_review_decide` (blocking-to-indeterminate array migration when any prerequisite record is indeterminate) are live production code path modifications with no stated rollback path, feature flag, or expand-contract sequencing.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'In orchestrator.finalize_verdict, add if coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE" before existing verdict branches' and 'after the single partition_findings call, if any final prerequisite coverage record is indeterminate, copy every entry from parts["blocking"] into parts["indeterminate"]'
scenarios: If the prerequisite coverage feature produces widespread spurious INDETERMINATE verdicts due to a prompt regression or upstream snapshot bug, there is no documented procedure to disable or roll back the verdict-enforcement logic without a full code revert and redeploy.
A bug in indeterminate propagation silently degrades all reviews that touch prerequisites until a fix is deployed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single boolean config/env flag (e.g. `PREREQUISITE_COVERAGE_ENFORCEMENT_ENABLED`, defaulting false in staging and true after a validated rollout) that short-circuits the new `if coverage.get('prerequisite_indeterminate')` branch and the blocking array migration; document the flag and the rollout sequence in the Implementation section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the `prerequisite_attribution_valid` veto semantics (record-level `indeterminate/attribution-invalid` conversion on `no` or `na`) as a co-located docstring or constant adjacent to the `_build_verification_output` extra-binary-fields argument or in the `plan_review_decide` decide-domain section, making the non-standard veto behavior explicit at the schema definition site.
evidence: Plan: 'Reuse the registered index-keyed `plan_review_verification` contract for focused Pass 2. Extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument and have only `plan_review_verification_model` pass `prerequisite_attribution_valid` with default `na`'
Plan: 'missing, duplicate, malformed, out-of-range, `no`, or `na` attribution converts that prerequisite record to `indeterminate/attribution-invalid` and removes its incompatibility findings'
Existing _build_binary pattern (verify_models.py lines 82-105): _BINARY_NA_DEFAULT and veto rules ARE co-located with the field definition

The `prerequisite_attribution_valid` veto applies a record-level structural mutation (converting an entire PrerequisiteCoverageRecord), not just a validity contribution — this is materially different from other binary veto fields and deserves explicit co-location
findings.finding: The `prerequisite_attribution_valid` veto trigger rule — specifically that a `no` or `na` value on this field converts the parent prerequisite record to `indeterminate/attribution-invalid` and removes its incompatibility findings — is defined in the `plan_review_decide` prose section, not co-located with the schema that defines the field. The field is added to `plan_review_verification_model` via `_build_verification_output`'s extra-binary-fields argument, but the model definition block contains no processing protocol; an implementer reading only the model code would not know that `no` triggers record-level conversion rather than being treated like any other binary veto.
findings.location: Implementation section — paragraph beginning 'In `plan_review_decide`, independently reshape and decide each domain'
scenarios: Implementer extends _build_verification_output correctly but writes the decide logic expecting `no` to reduce validity (as other binary vetoes do) rather than triggering full record-level conversion.
AC 'Missing or incorrect attribution cannot produce a blocking finding' cannot be verified locally from the schema definition.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `PlanRelationSnapshot` is a 'certified material-pin interface from `75de-02b0-d454-410b`' that already supplies one review-time preload with 'relation kind, readable state, pinned material, and ticket plan text' — but ticket `75de-02b0-d454-410b` is `status: open`, every test file in its manifest is `dep absent`, and no symbol named `PlanRelationSnapshot` exists anywhere in `src/`. The entire prerequisite-routing mechanism (preload keying, focused finder routing, isolation assertions) rests on this undelivered contract.
findings.location: Upstream contract section; Implementation section ('Assert exactly one relation preload supplies both pin creation and prerequisite routing')
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/relation_snapshot.py` (or equivalent) to this story's file_impact and define a minimal `PlanRelationSnapshot` stub (fields: `canonical_id`, `relation_kind`, `readable_state`, `pinned_material`, `plan_text`) so the prerequisite-finder can be implemented and tested independently of `75de`/`acaf`'s delivery.

findings.finding: The plan states 'Determine `execution_review` once from the review-phase snapshot supplied by `5967` and pass the same boolean to both general and focused `pass3_over_findings(..., execution_review=execution_review)` calls'. But ticket `5967-4476-7f9f-442d` is `status: open` (all its test files are `dep absent`), and the current `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings(findings, verifs)` takes exactly two positional arguments with no `execution_review` parameter. This story cannot call `pass3_over_findings(..., execution_review=execution_review)` without `5967` being merged first.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Determine execution_review once from the review-phase snapshot supplied by 5967 and pass the same boolean to both general and focused pass3_over_findings(..., execution_review=execution_review) calls, applying the 0.8 execution floor consistently.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command to the Verification section that explicitly runs the existing container corpus against parent/child criteria (e.g., `python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --container` or a named pytest marker) and link it to the 'Existing parent and child criteria retain existing container corpus results' AC.
evidence: The plan adds new optional fields to `parity.ItemRecord` and a new `prerequisite_fidelity_report` function. It states 'Preserve existing fields and container behavior. Existing container corpus results remain unchanged.'

Verification section commands: `pytest tests/unit/test_plan_review.py`, `make check`, `pytest -m 'not integration'` — none explicitly names a container corpus parity command or a test file that targets this AC
findings.finding: The acceptance criterion 'Existing parent and child criteria retain existing container corpus results' has no dedicated proving command in the Verification section. The general `make check` and `pytest -m 'not integration'` catch new failures but do not explicitly exercise the existing container corpus against parent/child criteria to prove the parity claim. If no existing test already does this, the AC is unverified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Existing parent and child criteria retain existing container corpus results'; Verification section
scenarios: A refactor of `parity.ItemRecord` or `parity_report` silently changes container corpus scoring. `make check` passes because unit tests do not cover the corpus path. The AC is marked complete with no evidence.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Promote the signal-chain test acceptance criterion into the main Acceptance Criteria list (e.g. 'Both `test_prerequisite_indeterminate_signal_chain_agentic` and `test_prerequisite_indeterminate_signal_chain_single_turn` pass against the production `plan-review.yaml`') and remove the duplicate embedded criterion from the Binding section.
evidence: Binding signal-chain and identity tests: 'Acceptance criterion: both production-YAML signal-chain tests pass, proving neither workflow branch can silently drop prerequisite coverage before final verdict assembly.'
Binding signal-chain and identity tests: 'Run `.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/unit/workflow/test_plan_review_workflow.py -k prerequisite_indeterminate_signal_chain` and require two passing tests.'
ABSENCE: Neither the signal-chain test names nor the embedded acceptance criterion appear in the 14-item main Acceptance Criteria list.
findings.finding: The signal-chain tests `test_prerequisite_indeterminate_signal_chain_agentic` and `test_prerequisite_indeterminate_signal_chain_single_turn` have their own embedded acceptance criterion and run command inside Binding signal-chain and identity tests, but are absent from the main Acceptance Criteria list. This creates two parallel and inconsistent completeness-verification structures — the main ACs and a separate embedded AC — making story-close review ambiguous about which list is authoritative.
findings.location: Binding signal-chain and identity tests section (embedded 'Acceptance criterion') vs Acceptance Criteria section
scenarios: Story-close reviewer checks only the main ACs, finds all 14 green, and marks the story done without verifying that the signal-chain tests exist and pass, leaving the workflow-branch coverage gap undetected.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a step to the implementation sequence requiring an ADR or AGENTS.md section update documenting the two-domain index design, the prerequisite-consistency pipeline stage, and the verdict-enforcement mechanism before the PR is merged.
evidence: Plan describes 'two non-overlapping verification index domains', 'focused finder route', 'focused verifier', 'focused Pass 2', 'independently reshape and decide each domain', and a new `prerequisite_indeterminate` signal chain — none of which are mentioned in any doc-update step.
No AGENTS.md, ADR, or design-doc update appears anywhere in Implementation, Acceptance Criteria, Verification, or Binding implementation sequence sections.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern — a two-domain LLM index system, a prerequisite-consistency pipeline stage embedded in an existing review workflow, and a new verdict-enforcement mechanism — with no mention of capturing this decision in an ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update.
findings.location: Implementation section (whole section) and Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: A future maintainer adding a third domain or modifying the index-domain merge logic has no authoritative source explaining why the domains are kept separate, what the invariants are, or why the prerequisite check is isolated from general criteria.
Onboarding engineers must reverse-engineer the two-domain architecture from tests and implementation rather than consulting a design doc.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify placing `PrerequisiteBlock` in `sizing.py` or move it to a dedicated types/prerequisite module and have `sizing.py` import it from there.
evidence: The plan states 'Finder, verifier, token estimation, stable sorting, and split recovery use this same type' — multiple orchestration components depend on a type that lives in a sizing utility.

No justification is given for placing a domain dataclass in sizing.py rather than a dedicated types or prerequisite module; this couples all consumers of PrerequisiteBlock to the sizing module's import graph.
findings.finding: `PrerequisiteBlock` — a domain-level concept representing a prerequisite plan for LLM consumption — is defined in `sizing.py`, coupling a domain/orchestration concern directly into the token-sizing utility module without justification.
findings.location: Binding prerequisite block and verifier prompt section — 'Define @dataclass(frozen=True) class PrerequisiteBlock in sizing.py'
scenarios: Adding a field to PrerequisiteBlock for orchestration purposes (e.g. a cached hash or relation metadata) forces changes inside the sizing module, which should be concerned only with token budgets.
Circular import risk if sizing.py later needs to import from orchestration modules that also import PrerequisiteBlock from sizing.py.
findings.suggested_fix: Define `PrerequisiteBlock` in a new `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/prerequisite_types.py` (or alongside the existing plan-review domain types) and import it into `sizing.py` for token-estimation use only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Instead of adding an optional-parameter escape hatch to `_build_verification_output`, define `_prerequisite_verification_model()` as a local factory in `passes.py` (following `_pass2_completion_model`) that builds its own Pydantic model with `prerequisite_attribution_valid`; register it under a separate contract name (e.g. `plan_review_prerequisite_verification`) rather than reusing `plan_review_verification` with a conditional field.
evidence: Existing `_build_verification_output` at `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py:108` has exactly 3 callers (`verification_model`, `plan_review_verification_model`, `code_review_verification_model`) and no optional parameters today.
Plan states `verification_model` and `code_review_verification_model` pass no extra field — only `plan_review_verification_model` uses it.
Established pattern for plan-review-only fields: `_pass2_completion_model()` at `passes.py:163` is a LOCAL factory that defines its shape without touching the shared kernel builder.
Rule of Three: only ONE caller would use the new parameter, making it a premature abstraction on a shared utility.
findings.finding: The plan proposes extending the shared `_build_verification_output` builder in `verify_models.py` with an optional `extra-binary-fields` argument so that only `plan_review_verification_model` can inject `prerequisite_attribution_valid` — a one-caller escape hatch on a 3-caller shared utility. The plan's own established pattern for gate-specific field extensions (used for `_pass1_model`, `_pass4_model`, `_pass2_completion_model`, `_pass2_completion_model`) is to define a LOCAL factory function that calls the shared builder internally, keeping the shared builder's signature stable.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Reuse the registered index-keyed `plan_review_verification` contract for focused Pass 2. Extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument...'
scenarios: A future story that calls `_build_verification_output` directly must know to pass or not pass the optional arg, creating a hidden coupling.
The shared builder's signature becomes entangled with one gate's optional veto field, making it harder to reason about the builder's contract boundaries.
findings.suggested_fix: Define a standalone `_prerequisite_verification_model()` factory in `passes.py` following the `_pass2_completion_model` precedent. It can include the `prerequisite_attribution_valid: str` field directly in its own `Verification` shape. Register under `plan_review_prerequisite_verification`. This avoids touching `_build_verification_output` and keeps the shared kernel clean.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py` (specifically the `verification_model` and `code_review_verification_model` call sites of `_build_verification_output`) to the explicit list of files requiring review/update when the optional extra-fields argument is added.
evidence: Plan: 'Extend _build_verification_output with an optional extra-binary-fields argument and have only plan_review_verification_model pass prerequisite_attribution_valid'
Plan: 'verification_model and code_review_verification_model pass no extra field and remain schema-identical'

verify_models.py lines 180 and 350: both verification_model() and code_review_verification_model() call _build_verification_output(forbid, SeverityAttrs/CodeSeverityAttrs) — these call sites are in review_kernel, outside plan_review/
ABSENCE: the plan does not enumerate these two callers in its file_impact or list them as edit targets
findings.finding: _build_verification_output in src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py is modified to accept an optional extra-binary-fields argument, but its two existing call sites outside plan_review — verification_model() and code_review_verification_model() — are not enumerated as consumers requiring an explicit update (even a no-op 'pass nothing' update).
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Reuse the registered index-keyed plan_review_verification contract for focused Pass 2. Extend _build_verification_output with an optional extra-binary-fields argument' / src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py
scenarios: If a future refactor makes the extra-binary-fields argument required, verification_model and code_review_verification_model would break without being in the plan's explicit edit list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the Implementation section explaining why full isolation (separate prompts, contracts, index domains, verify pass) is required — specifically, that it is the only approach that can satisfy the measurable isolation contract tests (no prerequisite IDs in general prompts) while reusing the existing verification pipeline.
evidence: No 'rationale' or 'why this approach' section exists in the plan

The isolation requirement is stated as an AC ('General finder, general verifier, and coach requests contain no prerequisite IDs or plan text') but its architectural implication (a completely separate pipeline) is never justified positively
findings.finding: The plan provides extensive implementation detail but offers no positive rationale for why a fully isolated, dual-index-domain, dual-pass-3 approach was chosen over structurally simpler alternatives. The isolation ACs are stated as requirements but not as the *reason* the architecture takes this form.
findings.location: Implementation section (entire), 'Approach soundness' is absent as a named section
scenarios: An implementer wonders whether the isolated index domains are strictly necessary or a stylistic choice, and makes a shortcut that leaks prerequisite IDs into general finders — invalidating the isolation contract tests — because no positive rationale made the requirement's importance clear.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the custom TLV+SHA-256 binary encoding for prerequisite-scoped finding IDs against established alternatives (e.g., UUID v5 with a registered namespace, SHA-256 over canonical JSON), specify the truncation length and its collision-safety guarantee, and document the policy for future scoped ID types to avoid a growing set of coexisting encodings.
evidence: 'encode four fields as UTF-8, prefix each with a 4-byte big-endian unsigned byte length, and concatenate them in this order: fixed version string `prerequisite-finding-id-v1`, finding text, comma-joined sorted criteria, and authoritative prerequisite ID. Hash the concatenated bytes with SHA-256 and retain the existing ID truncation.'
'Preserve `mint_finding_id` byte-for-byte for every finding without `prerequisite_id`'
ABSENCE: no comparison to UUID v5 (RFC 4122 §4.3), BLAKE3, or deterministic JSON hashing; no rationale for a hand-rolled TLV framing over an existing canonical serialization
findings.finding: The plan mandates a custom binary identity encoding (4-byte big-endian length-prefixed fields + SHA-256 truncation) for prerequisite-scoped findings, while non-prerequisite findings retain a pipe-delimited encoding — two coexisting encoding schemes with no justification for why an established deterministic ID scheme (e.g., UUID v5 with a namespace, or SHA-256 over canonical JSON) was not used instead.
findings.location: Binding signal-chain and identity tests (paragraph 1); Implementation section ('Extend `mint_finding_id`...'); Binding comparative baseline ('Preserve `mint_finding_id` byte-for-byte...')
scenarios: A reviewer implementing this must maintain two parallel encoding code paths indefinitely; any future third finding scope requires a third encoding decision with no established policy.

SHA-256 truncation length is unspecified in the plan; if truncation produces a short ID, cross-prerequisite collision probability is non-negligible and the plan's 'identical text from different prerequisites cannot deduplicate' guarantee is probabilistic, not structural — yet it is stated as a hard requirement.
findings.suggested_fix: Use UUID v5 with a fixed namespace UUID and a canonical JSON payload `{"v":"prerequisite-finding-id-v1","text":"...","criteria":[...],"prereq_id":"..."}` — this is a well-specified, collision-safe, zero-dependency deterministic ID that unifies with any future scoped type by varying only the namespace or payload version field.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `models_at_or_above()` exists in the codebase (module path and signature), confirm it returns models ordered by context-window size, and add an `upstream_contract` test that asserts the function is importable and satisfies the escalation contract before any focused-route code is written.
evidence: 'Escalate a singleton through `models_at_or_above()`. Largest-window exhaustion emits `indeterminate/evaluation-error` with `detail=input-too-large`.'

'focused verifier context-limit recovery uses the same whole-record algorithm as the finder: split the stable finding-record list into contiguous halves, retry independently, and escalate a singleton through `models_at_or_above()`'

ABSENCE: no module path, no import reference, no test that verifies the function signature; the plan's `pack_prerequisite_bins` and `sizing.py` sections name the module but do not cite `models_at_or_above` there
findings.finding: The plan relies on `models_at_or_above()` as the escalation primitive for singleton context-limit recovery in both finder and verifier paths, but does not verify that this function exists in the codebase, confirm its call signature, or justify that it is capable of returning an ordered list of models with strictly larger context windows.
findings.location: Implementation section ('Escalate a singleton through `models_at_or_above()`'); Binding focused prompt and failure contracts ('escalate a singleton through `models_at_or_above()`')
scenarios: If `models_at_or_above()` does not exist or returns models in an unspecified order, the escalation path silently fails, and a singleton that exceeds the initial model's context window receives `evaluation-error` instead of attempting escalation.

If the function exists but accepts a different signature (e.g., takes a capability enum rather than a model string), implementation drift causes a runtime error in the recovery path that is difficult to catch in unit tests without the live function.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the runner-to-disposition mapping rule (parse-invalid/schema-invalid/unknown-ID → `output-invalid`; provider failure → `evaluation-error`) as a co-located comment or constant block adjacent to `_prerequisite_coverage_model()` in `passes.py`, matching the established pattern of COMPLETION_CONTAINMENT_CLOSED/defaults near _pass2_completion_model().
evidence: Plan defines model factory: 'Define `_prerequisite_coverage_model()` in `passes.py`'
Plan separately states: 'Missing, duplicate, unknown-ID, parse-invalid, or schema-invalid output maps unresolved IDs in the bin to `output-invalid`; provider failure maps them to `evaluation-error`'
The `reason_code` field is part of the model schema but its population rule is in a different prose paragraph with no cross-reference
Existing pattern in passes.py: COMPLETION_CONTAINMENT_CLOSED and fail-safe defaults are co-located with the _pass2_completion_model() (lines 149-160) — this story breaks that pattern
findings.finding: The processing rule mapping runner-side failure states to the `disposition`/`reason_code` fields of `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord` is not co-located with the `_prerequisite_coverage_model()` schema definition. The rule ('Missing, duplicate, unknown-ID, parse-invalid, or schema-invalid output maps unresolved IDs in the bin to `output-invalid`; provider failure maps them to `evaluation-error`') appears only in a prose paragraph after the model definition, not inside or adjacent to the model factory function that implementers will extend. Any executor implementing `_prerequisite_coverage_model()` must search separately to find the rule that governs when `reason_code` is populated.
findings.location: Implementation section — paragraph beginning 'Require exactly one record per readable pinned canonical ID'
scenarios: Implementer writes _prerequisite_coverage_model() without noticing the separate mapping rule and produces a runner that emits indeterminate records with empty reason_code on schema-invalid output.
The test for 'indeterminate records never assert incompatibility' (AC) passes structurally but the wrong reason_code is silently emitted.

findings.finding: The 'Binding implementation sequence' section states 'The concrete first- and second-slice proving command is `.venv/bin/python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing`' (identical for both slices), while the 'Binding comparative baseline' section specifies a different first-slice command (`--prerequisite-packing --singleton --write-baseline src/...`) and a different second-slice command (`--prerequisite-packing --baseline src/...`). An executor cannot determine which command to run as the gate for each slice.
findings.location: Binding implementation sequence (step 1, final paragraph) vs. Binding comparative baseline (first and second paragraphs)
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Binding implementation sequence' step 1 final paragraph, replace the single-command sentence with: 'The first-slice proving command is `python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing --singleton --write-baseline src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/plan-review-prerequisite-packing.baseline.json`; the second-slice proving command is `python -m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval --prerequisite-packing --baseline src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/plan-review-prerequisite-packing.baseline.json`.'  Remove the conflicting identical-command claim.

findings.finding: The Implementation section describes the `finalize_verdict` modification as only inserting `verdict = 'INDETERMINATE'` (with `elif` rewrites for existing branches), but the 'Binding comparative baseline' section additionally requires moving every otherwise-blocking finding into the `indeterminate` array with `decision=indeterminate` and `reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate` and clearing `blocking`. These two descriptions of the same function's behavior are split across sections with no cross-reference, so an implementer following only the Implementation section will produce a verdict string change without the required array manipulation.
findings.location: Implementation section ('orchestrator.finalize_verdict' paragraph) vs. Binding comparative baseline section ('When prerequisite coverage is indeterminate...' paragraph)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section's `finalize_verdict` paragraph, append: 'Also move every finding in the `blocking` array into the `indeterminate` array with `decision=indeterminate` and `reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate`, then clear `blocking`, before returning.' Add a back-reference note in the Binding comparative baseline section: 'See Implementation section for the complete finalize_verdict change.'

findings.finding: The plan specifies that blocking findings are moved into the `indeterminate` array with `decision=indeterminate` when `prerequisite_indeterminate` is true (Binding comparative baseline: 'move every otherwise-blocking finding into the `indeterminate` array with `decision=indeterminate` and `reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate`'). However, `finalize_verdict` currently reads a *pre-partitioned* `parts` dict produced by `partition_findings` — blocking findings already sit in `parts['blocking']` before `finalize_verdict` runs. The plan also specifies that `plan_review_decide` converts attribution-invalid prerequisite records and 'removes its incompatibility findings'. These two descriptions create an ambiguous split: it is unclear whether the array mutation (moving findings to `indeterminate`) happens in `plan_review_decide` before `partition_findings`, or inside `finalize_verdict` after the partition. An implementer reading only the `finalize_verdict` modification section could change only the verdict string (adding the `if coverage.get('prerequisite_indeterminate')` branch) without mutating `parts['blocking']`, producing a verdict of INDETERMINATE whose `blocking` array still contains findings — an inconsistent output that the Binding comparative baseline says must not happen.
findings.location: Implementation section ('This story explicitly modifies `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`') and Binding comparative baseline ('move every otherwise-blocking finding into the `indeterminate` array')
findings.suggested_fix: Assign the blocking-finding movement to `plan_review_decide`: after both general and focused decide calls, if any focused record is `indeterminate`, re-classify all focused findings with `decision='block'` to `decision='indeterminate'` with `reason='prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate'` before the single `partition_findings` call. `finalize_verdict` then only needs to add the verdict-string branch (`if coverage.get('prerequisite_indeterminate'): verdict='INDETERMINATE'`) without mutating `parts`.

findings.finding: The behavior of `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` when `prerequisite_indeterminate` is true — specifically, relocating every otherwise-blocking finding to the indeterminate array with `decision=indeterminate` and `reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate` — is specified only in 'Binding comparative baseline and compatibility behavior', not in the 'Implementation' section's description of the `finalize_verdict` change. An executor following only the Implementation section would implement the verdict-string flip and signing boundary but omit the finding relocation, producing an inconsistent response (verdict=INDETERMINATE with non-empty blocking array).
findings.location: Implementation section: 'immediately after extracting partition lists and before the current `if blocking` branch, add `if coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE"`'; Binding comparative baseline: 'move every otherwise-blocking finding into the `indeterminate` array with decision=indeterminate and reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate, clear blocking, then return verdict INDETERMINATE'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section's finalize_verdict paragraph, add after the verdict assignment: 'Also move every entry from the blocking list to the indeterminate list, setting decision=indeterminate and reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate, and clear blocking, so that verdict string and arrays remain consistent.' Then remove the duplicate description from Binding comparative baseline or add an explicit cross-reference.

findings.finding: The Implementation section's `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` modification says only 'add `if coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate"): verdict = "INDETERMINATE"` … with existing branches changed to `elif`' — it says nothing about moving blocking findings to the indeterminate array or clearing the blocking array. The Binding comparative baseline section separately specifies: 'move every otherwise-blocking finding into the indeterminate array with decision=indeterminate and reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate, clear blocking, then return verdict INDETERMINATE.' An implementer following only the Implementation section will set the correct verdict string but leave blocking findings in the blocking array, contradicting the Binding comparative baseline behavior contract.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'This story explicitly modifies orchestrator.finalize_verdict'; Binding comparative baseline section — 'When prerequisite coverage is indeterminate, move every otherwise-blocking finding...'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section under 'This story explicitly modifies orchestrator.finalize_verdict', extend the description to read: 'add `if coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate"): move every otherwise-blocking finding into the indeterminate array with decision=indeterminate and reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate, clear blocking, then set verdict = "INDETERMINATE"`, with existing branches changed to `elif`.' Remove the redundant restatement from Binding comparative baseline.

findings.finding: The focused Pass-2 verifier prompt is never named or specified. The plan names the finder prompt file (`src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_prerequisite_finder.md`, prompt ID `plan-review-prerequisite-finder`) and defines `PASS_PREREQUISITE_FINDER`, but the focused verifier prompt — which receives 'the subject plan and, for each local finding, its authoritative prerequisite ID and plan block from the preload' — has no named file, no prompt ID constant (`PASS_PREREQUISITE_VERIFIER` is declared but only its name is given), and no instructions specification. The plan says 'The focused verifier receives the subject plan and, for each local finding...' but never specifies the system prompt content, file path, or prompt template.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add `PASS_PREREQUISITE_FINDER = "plan-review-prerequisite-finder"` and `PASS_PREREQUISITE_VERIFIER = "plan-review-prerequisite-verifier"` in `passes.py`' and 'The focused verifier receives the subject plan and, for each local finding...'

findings.finding: The `PrerequisiteBlock` type used throughout the plan (in `pack_prerequisite_bins` signature, the finder user input construction, and focused verifier record assembly) is never defined. The plan defines `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord` and `PrerequisiteCoverageOutput` but never defines a `PrerequisiteBlock` dataclass or TypedDict, even though it is referenced with a `.rendered_text` attribute in the `pack_prerequisite_bins` sizing section and implicitly in the finder's block-delimiter framing.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Define `pack_prerequisite_bins`(blocks: Sequence[PrerequisiteBlock], ...)' and 'Binding implementation sequence step 4'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `sizing.py` (or a shared types module): `@dataclass(frozen=True) class PrerequisiteBlock: canonical_id: str; rendered_text: str` — and reference it from `pack_prerequisite_bins` and the finder/verifier call sites.

findings.finding: `PrerequisiteBlock` is named in the `pack_prerequisite_bins` signature (charging `est_tokens(block.rendered_text) + per_block_output_tokens` per block) and in the `_prerequisite_coverage_model` runtime, but its field structure is never defined anywhere in the plan. The field `rendered_text` is assumed but not declared; whether the struct also carries `canonical_id`, `relation_kind`, `pinned_material`, `ticket_plan_text`, or other upstream contract fields is unspecified.
findings.location: Binding implementation sequence step 4: 'pack_prerequisite_bins(blocks: Sequence[PrerequisiteBlock], …) … Charge each whole block est_tokens(block.rendered_text) + per_block_output_tokens.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a definition such as: 'PrerequisiteBlock is a dataclass with fields canonical_id: str (the authoritative canonical ticket ID), rendered_text: str (the concatenated plan text from PlanRelationSnapshot, used for token estimation and prompt inclusion), and relation_kind: str.' Specify exactly how rendered_text is composed from the upstream snapshot fields.

findings.finding: The plan specifies 'Extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument and have only `plan_review_verification_model` pass `prerequisite_attribution_valid`' but does not specify whether this extra field is added to the `Binary` sub-model (built by `_build_binary`) or directly to the `Verification` wrapper model. The current `_build_verification_output` calls `_build_binary(forbid)` to produce the Binary type, then passes it as a field of Verification. Adding a field to Binary vs. to Verification has different semantic and schema implications: Binary fields participate in the graded vocabulary enforcement; Verification-level fields do not. The plan says the field is 'a plan-review-only veto rather than a validity contributor' and is 'excluded from `GRADED_BINARY`' — which implies it should be in Binary (not in Verification), but the instruction 'Do not add the field to shared `_build_binary`' means a separate Binary-extension mechanism is needed. Without specifying where exactly the field lives in the output model, an implementer could place it at the wrong level, making `code_review_verification_model` and `verification_model` schema-identical to `plan_review_verification_model` in the wrong way.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Reuse the registered index-keyed `plan_review_verification` contract for focused Pass 2. Extend `_build_verification_output` with an optional extra-binary-fields argument...'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one named end-to-end acceptance test (or integration test in `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_gate.py`) that exercises the full new prerequisite path through the production `plan-review.yaml` — from a real `PlanRelationSnapshot` (or a faithful fixture) through focused finder, verifier, `plan_review_decide` (both domains), and `finalize_verdict` — asserting the correct verdict and prerequisite coverage records at the terminal output; or explicitly defer this to a named child ticket.
evidence: 'load the production plan-review.yaml, supplies a fake focused decide result' — signal-chain tests bypass the LLM evaluation steps
Fidelity eval commands (--prerequisite-packing) test finder quality against a gold corpus but do not route through plan_review_decide or the workflow YAML
No named test exercises: real PlanRelationSnapshot → focused finder → focused verifier → plan_review_decide (both domains) → partition_findings → finalize_verdict end-to-end
No child ticket named to defer this coverage; no documented rationale explaining why the fake/offline substitute is faithful to the production path
findings.finding: The full end-to-end new default pipeline — snapshot preload → focused finder LLM call → focused verifier LLM call → plan_review_decide (focused domain) → verdict → plan-review.yaml terminal node — is not exercised by any named live/end-to-end acceptance criterion. The signal-chain tests load the production YAML but inject a 'fake focused decide result,' bypassing the evaluation steps entirely. The fidelity spot eval exercises only finder prompt quality against a gold corpus, not the decide integration, verifier wiring, or verdict enforcement. For any plan that has prerequisites, this is the newly defaulted path, yet the acceptance criteria are satisfiable while the wiring from evaluate → decide → verdict remains untested with real outputs.
findings.location: Verification section; Binding signal-chain and identity tests; AC line 'Every upheld focused finding appears in the verdict with its authoritative prerequisite ID'
scenarios: Wiring bug in plan_review_decide focused-domain reshaping (e.g., index-domain confusion, wrong attribution-invalid promotion) would be invisible to all current tests — signal-chain tests bypass decide; unit tests use mocks
plan-review.yaml YAML wiring error connecting focused verifier output arrays to plan_review_decide input would not be caught by signal-chain (fake input) or fidelity eval (doesn't run the YAML)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an acceptance criterion: 'An integration test in `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_plan_review_gate.py` exercises the focused prerequisite path end-to-end using a fixture `PlanRelationSnapshot` (not a fake decide output), confirms focused findings surface in verdict arrays with correct `prerequisite_id`, and confirms an indeterminate record produces an unsigned INDETERMINATE verdict through the production YAML.' Alternatively, extend the second-slice fidelity proving command to route through the full workflow pipeline rather than the finder-only path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `gate_dispatch._recover_plan_review_after_verify_failure` as a caller of `pass3_over_findings` and `finalize_verdict` that must also thread `execution_review` and, if prerequisite coverage is available from a succeeded step, pass `prerequisite_indeterminate` — or explicitly document why neither applies on the verify-failure degraded path.
evidence: Plan states 'P8 short-circuit and drift_refresh call sites pass no prerequisite coverage by design; coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate") is false on those paths' — names exactly two sites but omits gate_dispatch._recover_plan_review_after_verify_failure
gate_dispatch.py line 375: decided = orchestrator.pass3_over_findings(pass1, {})
gate_dispatch.py line 396: verdict = orchestrator.finalize_verdict(pctx, parts, coaching=[], coverage=coverage, ...)
5967 ticket confirms pass3_over_findings will gain execution_review parameter — verify-failure recovery must also pass it or use the false default; the plan is silent on this
findings.finding: gate_dispatch.py's verify-failure recovery path (_recover_plan_review_after_verify_failure, line ~375) calls orchestrator.pass3_over_findings and orchestrator.finalize_verdict but is not enumerated as a consumer site requiring updates. The plan names 'P8 short-circuit and drift_refresh call sites' as explicitly not passing prerequisite coverage, but the verify-failure recovery site is a third caller of both functions that also will not receive execution_review or prerequisite_indeterminate coverage — and it is not acknowledged.
findings.location: Implementation section / 'Binding operational behavior' / src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py lines ~370-399
scenarios: After this story ships, a ticket in execution phase whose Pass-2 verify fails will hit the recovery path, which calls pass3_over_findings without execution_review=True — silently using planning-phase thresholds even for an execution-phase ticket.

A prerequisite record indeterminate during a verify-failure run would not be reflected in finalize_verdict since the recovery path builds its own coverage dict without the prerequisite_indeterminate key.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `gate_dispatch.py` to the 'Binding operational behavior' acknowledged-caller list alongside drift_refresh and P8 short-circuit, and state explicitly that on a verify-failure path execution_review defaults false (planning) and prerequisite_indeterminate is absent (treated as false), with rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_workflow_schema_golden.py` to the Verification section and implementation edit-target list, confirming whether schema extensions to plan_review_decide_input, plan_review_decide_output, and plan_review_coach_input require golden updates.
evidence: 5967 ticket signature manifest includes: 'dep ... tests/interfaces/contracts/test_workflow_schema_golden.py' — this test guards plan_review_decide_input and plan_review_precheck_output schema contracts
Plan extends plan_review_decide_input.schema.json, plan_review_decide_output.schema.json, and plan_review_coach_input.schema.json with new optional/required fields

Plan's Verification section names only test_plan_review.py, test_plan_review_prerequisites.py, test_plan_review_workflow.py, test_plan_review_prerequisite_isolation.py, test_plan_review_gate.py — omits test_workflow_schema_golden.py
findings.finding: The plan names new tests `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_plan_review_prerequisite_isolation.py` but does not name the existing test file `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_workflow_schema_golden.py` as a consumer requiring update, even though the plan extends plan_review_decide_input.schema.json (adding optional has_prerequisites, prerequisite_coverage, prerequisite_findings, prerequisite_verifications) and extends decide output and coach input schemas — and the schema golden test is shown in 5967's attestation manifest as a verified contract guard for those exact schemas.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add optional has_prerequisites... to plan_review_decide_input.schema.json... Extend decide output and coach input schemas' / tests/interfaces/contracts/test_workflow_schema_golden.py
scenarios: If test_workflow_schema_golden.py pins exact schema shapes (required arrays, additionalProperties postures) and these schemas are extended, the golden test may fail or need updating — and the plan doesn't acknowledge it as an edit target.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether findings that migrate from `blocking` to `indeterminate` due to `prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate` should receive coaching notes; if yes, extend the `coachable` assembly in `plan_review_coach` to also include indeterminate findings carrying that reason code.
evidence: 'copy every entry from parts["blocking"] into parts["indeterminate"] with decision=indeterminate and reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate, then clear parts["blocking"]'
In plan_review_coach (workflow_ops.py:528): 'coachable = blocking_in + surviving' — findings in indeterminate are excluded from coaching
The plan says 'retains all findings for remediation and UI display' but does not mention coaching for demoted findings
findings.finding: The plan demotes formerly-blocking findings to the `indeterminate` array when prerequisite coverage is indeterminate, but `plan_review_coach` builds `coachable = blocking_in + surviving` — findings that moved from `blocking` to `indeterminate` never receive coach notes. The plan states 'retains all findings for remediation and UI display' but does not address the coaching gap, leaving previously-blocking findings uncoached and harder to remediate in the INDETERMINATE case.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'In plan_review_decide, after the single partition_findings call, if any final prerequisite coverage record is indeterminate, copy every entry from parts["blocking"] into parts["indeterminate"]...' and workflow_ops.plan_review_coach coachable assembly
scenarios: A legitimate blocking incompatibility finding exists. Prerequisite coverage is indeterminate (e.g. LLM context-limit on verification). The finding moves to indeterminate — it is visible but receives no coaching note, leaving the plan author without actionable guidance.
findings.suggested_fix: In `plan_review_coach`, after assembling `coachable = blocking_in + surviving`, extend it: `coachable += [f for f in ctx.inputs.get('indeterminate', []) if f.get('reason') == 'prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate']`. Alternatively, state explicitly in the Implementation section that demoted findings intentionally receive no coaching in this case and why.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
